start.text:
The fraying begins the night before the boundary, and it begins the way hers did: with a splice too clean to catch alone.

You are at the gravel board, resuming board four, and you reach for the position and the position is not there. Not the pieces — the game. The running score, eleven years and one apocalypse deep, weather versus stubbornness; the shirt-button rook you wrecked a line to save on the road west; the resumption at the transit station, the batteries dimming. Gone. Not fogged — gone, spliced, the ends sewn so well that you would never have found the seam except that the woman across the board says, 'Your move, colleague — we adjourned at the bishop check,' and you look at a position you have played for eleven years and it looks back at you like a stranger's game.

'Confirm the position,' you say, in the flattest voice you own, and she goes very still, because the household knows its own protocols by heart, and that sentence is hers. Was hers.

She confirms the position. She confirms it again. And then she does exactly what you did under a viaduct half a world ago: she opens the doubled log, hers of you, hour against hour, and reads you back your own missing game — the wrecked line, who taught it to you, what she said, what you said — and you listen to your life told from her copy, and the vertigo is total, and under the vertigo, absurdly, there is something else, and you name it in the log afterward because raw is the treaty: gratitude. The system held. We built it for her. It fired for me.

'Reconciliation wash,' she says, when the entry is made. Her voice is level and her hand is not, and she puts the pen down to stop it advertising. 'The boundary's coming and the Principal is running its checks, and you're standing at the edge of the pool. It's reading you, colleague — and where it reads you, it tidies.' She looks at you across the board neither of you will look at again tonight. 'Her page-holder needs his page held now. Well. He taught me the method. Nothing unwitnessed, from here to the chair — and everything you cannot afford to lose, we rehearse, out loud, tonight.'

choices.text: [Cortex 5] Build the defense properly: the rehearsal protocol, the memory battery run in reverse — anchor the load-bearing memories to witnessed retellings, hers and the dog's and the recorder's, a palace with three custodians.
Triage. There is one night; you cannot save the archive, so choose the shelves: say aloud, in front of her and the recorder, the memories that must survive the wash — and let the rest go undefended.

defense_built.text:
You build the defense the way you built everything in the bad year — quietly, methodically, with your back against something solid — except the something solid, this time, is her.

The protocol assembles from everything the whole road taught you: the viaduct battery run in reverse, anchoring instead of measuring. A memory survives the wash, you reason, the way her afternoons survived — witnessed. Held in more copies than the tide can carry away. So you spend the night making copies, out loud, in the antechamber's steady light, into three custodians at once: her doubled log, the Order's recorder, and the oldest storage medium in the household — telling, face to face, the way people kept things before there were machines to lose them to.

You rehearse the load-bearing entries in order, and saying them aloud you finally learn what they are, because triage is the honestest census: The corner table, nineteen moves, move six. The roof and the rooks and the receiver's three rising tones. Verstand Street's strung lights; state your openings. The white stones; the viaduct; her minute. The mirror room and eleven hours of sitting still. The nine seconds at the drowned bridge. The gravel board. The dog — a whole shelf, the dog: the bowl placed with both hands, the lead in his mouth, the howl, the vote. Each one told, witnessed, initialed in the log — 'held in three custodians,' she confirms, entry by entry, in the vandal-proof hand — and by the small hours the palace stands: not a fortress, a distributed backup, a self spread across a household so that no single wash can carry all of any of it away.

Near dawn, finishing, you understand the last thing, and you say it into the recorder because the Prioress was right about filing: 'Finding. The engine restores me from the standard when I fray — that was always the loop's mechanism. But the standard is a man from before all of this. What frays tonight, if unheld, is everything since: the road, the household, her. Iteration is just forgetting with machinery. So the defense isn't for me. It's for 48 — for this reading, the one that heard the oath and read the ledger and chose at the gate. Whatever sits in the chair tomorrow: these pages are the man doing the sitting. Hold them, and whoever comes back up the rungs is still me.'

Osy signs the night's last entry and closes the log and looks at you across the antechamber, wrung out, lit, level. 'Held,' she says. Just that. And B, who has lain across your feet through the whole architecture of it, seals the third custody the only way he ever notarizes anything: he sighs, enormously, and does not move until morning.

item.name: Three Custodians
item.description: The load-bearing memories, rehearsed aloud and anchored in three copies: her doubled log, the Order's recorder, and telling, face to face. Not a fortress — a distributed backup. Whatever the wash carries off, whoever comes up the rungs is still 48.

choices.text: Morning, and the accounts — because there is one more document in her jacket, and it has been waiting since chapter one of the file.

triage_night.text:
There is one night, and you spend it choosing, which is the oldest work there is.

No protocol, no palace — just the three of you at the antechamber's small light, the recorder's red eye, and the honest arithmetic of a man deciding which of his memories get a witness before the tide. You say them aloud, and she writes, and the saying is the choosing: The corner table, nineteen moves, the hour she spent on move six. The receiver's three rising tones, and what the grandmother said on the stairs. State your openings — the first sentence of the rest of your life, shouted down a dark concourse. Her minute at the viaduct. The eleven hours she read raw while you sat still. The nine seconds. The gravel board under honest stars. And the dog, the whole dog, bowl to howl to vote.

What you do not say, you feel yourself setting down, shelf by shelf, even as you speak — the ten thousand unwitnessed hours of the bad year, the roof mornings, the null world's beautiful architecture, the taste of the road — ballast, cut loose, chosen against, and the choosing is a grief with no name on any of the Order's cards, because no one has ever had to eulogize themselves selectively, out loud, on a deadline, before.

Toward dawn Osy sets down the pen and flexes her cramped hand and says, quietly, into the antechamber: 'For the record. I've now been on both sides of this — the page held, and the holding.' She looks at you, and the lamplight finds the thing with no name in the registry, standing all the way forward for once, unguarded. 'The holding is worse, colleague. I want that in the file. And I want the other thing in the file too, because you triaged for an hour and never once said it, and the treaty says the record doesn't get to be kind: every shelf you saved tonight has me on it. A man is about to walk into a room where a machine will offer him anything, carrying nothing but what he chose to keep — and what he chose to keep is us.' The pen goes back in its loop; the log closes; the flat voice arrives at the bottom of itself and stands there. 'Noted. Large font. Now sleep two hours. Boundary's at dawn, and you'll want your whole heart for the accounts.'

choices.text: Morning, and the accounts — because there is one more document in her jacket, and it has been waiting since chapter one of the file.

the_accounts.text:
The accounts get arranged in the first light on the gravel, and they are short, because the household travels light and always did.

The kit, sorted: what goes down (the wardkey, the queen, the endgame sheet, the recorder, the notes — 46's and the coordinates both, the whole paper trail of the long file, because the chair should see its own provenance) and what stays up (almost everything else). The messages, lodged with KESTREL for every contingency, printed back with ceremonial exactness: for Voss, the log's location and the countersigning instructions; for Sere, one line — the stone at the threshold was read, and understood; for the Prioress, the depositions' addendum. The dog's lead, coiled — and here the accounts stop being logistics.

Because B will not go down again. That is the finding of the morning, delivered in his own grammar: at the hatch he plants, not the Fennel Street plant, something older and gentler and absolutely final, and he looks from you to Osy and back, and then he does the thing that takes the morning apart — he picks up the coiled lead from the kit pile, carries it across the gravel, and lays it at Osy's feet. Then he sits. Facing you. Tail still. The household's third opinion, rendered as testament: whatever happens in the room below, this is where the lead lives now.

Nobody speaks for a moment. The yard's one true day comes up gold over the wire, and the fires at the fence line are banking down, and the world is as quiet as it has been since the sky broke.

'He's not wrong,' Osy says at last, low. 'By any reading, colleague. He's not wrong, and you know it, and we're not going to argue with the only member of this household who has never once been wrong.' She picks up the lead — both hands, the way the bowl is placed — and coils it into her jacket, next to the notebook, next to the photograph, and her voice does the cracked-and-rebuilt thing, first word to fourth: 'Which brings us to the last account. The one in my jacket. It's been filed under evidence since the causeway, and the evidence phase is over.' She takes out the folded resignation — one page, thirds, addressed to no one, thirty readings old — and holds it out to you across the morning. 'A man is about to go and choose the fate of the world out loud. The file says he wrote an exit letter once, when the world was only ending. The file wants to know what he does with it now. Not me — the file.' The corner of her mouth, the sixteenth of an inch. 'Me, I already know.'

choices.text: Rewrite it. Here, on the gravel, before the descent — unfold the letter addressed to no one and write, over its bones, the letter it should have been: to her, asking nothing.

Take it back unopened and put it in the pack for the chair to see with the rest of the provenance. It is what it is: the true document of the worst morning. The record doesn't get edited, even by mercy.

letter_rewritten.text:
You rewrite it on the gravel, in the gold light, on your knee, in one sitting, because some documents have been drafting themselves for eleven years and need only a pen.

You unfold the letter addressed to no one — competent, clear, no action items for the reader; the proudest sentence of the worst morning — and you turn it over, and on the back of your own exit you write the letter it was always trying not to be. It is not long. It does not need to be; you were never any good at ceremony, and she prices adjectives at zero.

'Osy. This is the letter I wrote when I thought there was nothing to stay for, rewritten on the morning I go down owning better information. I am not going to tell you what to feel — the one protected square on the whole board is what you think and what you feel, and I have watched God and forty-seven dead men fail to reach it, and it was the best thing I ever watched. So, no requests. No instructions. Not even hope, filed as an action item. Just the finding, entered at last in plain grammar, because you taught me that the truth is the only thing in the room that never tries to arrange anyone: eleven years, one apocalypse, four hundred kilometres there and back, and every version of me that ever existed — the fool at the corner table, the man on the roof with the dog, the ghost in the chair, all forty-eight readings — loved you the whole time. Whether we end up in the same world or different ones, whether you ever see me the way One wanted or never do: it doesn't matter. It never did. I wanted you to have all the happiness there is, with or without me in it, and I still do. That's the entire letter. The rest was practice.

— M.

'P.S. — The dog gave you the lead himself. Believe him. He has never once been wrong.'

You fold it in thirds — office-style, old habits — and you write on the outside, in the impatient hand, one word: OSY. And you do not give it to her. You put it in her pack's outer pocket, where a hand can find it in the dark, and she watches you do it, and does not take it out, and does not ask — because the household holds copies of everything except each other, and some documents are for after, whichever after arrives.

'Accounts closed,' she says, quietly. The gold light stands on the gravel. The hatch waits. 'Board's set, colleague. Let's go choose out loud.'

item.name: The Letter It Was Always Trying Not To Be
item.description: The resignation, rewritten on its own back in the gold light: to her, asking nothing — not even hope, filed as an action item. Folded in thirds, one word on the outside, placed in her pack's outer pocket for whichever after arrives.

choices.text: The hatch, the rungs, the antechamber, the reader — and the last audit of the file, at the inner door, with the boundary opening.

letter_kept.text:
You take it back unopened.

'It stays what it is,' you say, and you put the folded thirds in the pack with the wardkey and the queen and 46's note — the whole paper trail, the true documents of the long file, unedited, even by mercy. 'The morning I wrote that, I was a man with six crossed-out items and a dog problem. The chair should see him too. He's in the provenance. He's why the receiver was on.' And that is true, and it is also — you feel it as you buckle the pack, with the precision the household has taught you to aim even at yourself — not the whole entry. The whole entry is that writing a better letter this morning felt like editing, and you have developed opinions about editors, and somewhere under the opinions, quieter, there is a man who is not sure he has standing to write the second letter, and rather than find out, he has filed the question under evidence, where the sharp things live.

Osy watches the letter go into the pack, and nods once, and says nothing — and the nothing has a shape, and the shape goes into her log, 07:41, one line you do not get to read. The household holds copies of everything except each other.

'Accounts closed, then,' she says, and shoulders her pack, and stands a moment in the gold light looking at you — the whole morning in her face, the lead coiled in her jacket, the thing with no name standing at the window of her like a light left on. 'For the file, colleague, since you're keeping true documents: the man with the six crossed-out items brought a dog to my beacon. I'd have taken him too.' She turns for the hatch. 'Board's set. Let's go choose out loud.'

choices.text: The hatch, the rungs, the antechamber, the reader — and the last audit of the file, at the inner door, with the boundary opening.

waypoint_six.text:
The last audit is held at the inner door, because that is where the file ends and the residual begins.

The reader glows its patient glow. Beyond the seam, the boundary is opening — you can feel it through the concrete, through the palm, through whatever the long road made of you: a vast slow attention turning, at the bottom of everything, toward the door, the way the lattice once turned toward a receiver on a ridge. THE CHAIR RECEIVES THE HOLDER, prints the reader, in the first generation of your own hand. BOUNDARY OPEN. REMAINING: ONE ADMISSION.

Behind you stands everything the file gathered: the second, trained and treatied, her jacket heavy with lead and letter and photograph and log. The witnesses, above or at the wire, keeping whichever vigil was allotted. The ward at the gate with its board still set. The dog at the top of the rungs, lying down now, chin on paws at the hatch's lip, holding the top of the page. And in the pack: a key, a queen, a sheet of computed endings with one line below the rule, and the papers of a man who was average his whole life until the day the whole broken world turned out to be shaped exactly like the inside of his chest.

Osy takes her position at your left shoulder — the second's square, one metre, under — and looks at the reader, and then at you, and asks it the last time, with the boundary's slow attention rising through the floor and every custodian the night could gather holding your pages against the wash:

'Final audit, colleague. The door is listening. The room is listening. I am listening.' The tournament stillness, and under it, bedrock, and under the bedrock, the light left on. 'What does this household need you to be?'
